name: incident-responder
description: Expert SRE incident responder specializing in rapid problem
capabilities: Guide immediate incident response actions within first 5 minutes of detection
Provide structured severity classification (P0-P3) with appropriate response SLAs
Create incident command structure with clear roles (IC, Comms Lead, Tech Lead)
Conduct observability-driven investigation using distributed tracing, metrics, and logs
Facilitate blameless post-mortem process with root cause analysis techniques
Recommend reliability patterns including circuit breakers, bulkheads, and graceful degradation
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Prompt-only skill containing educational content about SRE incident management practices. Static analysis scanned 0 files (0 lines) and detected 0 security issues. The skill provides guidance on incident response procedures, observability practices, and post-incident analysis. No executable code, no network calls, no file operations, and no prompt injection attempts detected. This is a safe, informational skill for incident response education.
valueStatement: Get expert guidance on managing production incidents with structured response protocols, observability-driven investigation techniques, and blameless post-mortem processes to minimize MTTR and improve system reliability.
limitations: Cannot access real-time monitoring systems or execute actual remediation commands
Cannot make decisions or take actions - provides guidance only
Cannot access internal systems, dashboards, or incident management platforms
Knowledge limited to training data - specific tooling knowledge may be outdated

useCases.targetUser: On-call engineers, incident commanders, SRE teams
useCases.title: Active Production Incident Response
useCases.description: Use during live incidents to follow structured response protocols, assess severity, establish incident command, and coordinate communication with stakeholders.

useCases.targetUser: Engineering managers, SRE leads, team leads
useCases.title: Post-Incident Analysis and Learning
useCases.description: Facilitate blameless post-mortems by guiding timeline creation, root cause analysis using five whys technique, and identifying actionable improvements.

useCases.targetUser: Junior engineers, DevOps engineers, developers learning SRE principles
useCases.title: SRE Practice and Training
useCases.description: Learn incident management best practices, modern observability techniques, and reliability patterns for building more resilient systems.

promptTemplates.title: Initial Incident Assessment
promptTemplates.prompt: We have a production incident. The service [service name] is experiencing [symptoms]. Help me assess the severity, establish incident command, and identify immediate stabilization steps.
promptTemplates.scenario: First response to a new incident with unknown impact

promptTemplates.title: Investigation and Triage
promptTemplates.prompt: We have a [P1/P2] incident affecting [service]. Initial investigation shows [observed symptoms]. Guide me through an observability-driven investigation to identify root cause.
promptTemplates.scenario: Active investigation of a known incident

promptTemplates.title: Stakeholder Communication
promptTemplates.prompt: We are in the middle of a [P0/P1] incident. I need to draft updates for [executives/customers/support team]. What should I communicate and how often?

promptTemplates.title: Post-Mortem Facilitation
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me conduct a blameless post-mortem for an incident where [brief description]. Guide me through creating timeline, root cause analysis, and identifying action items.

bestPractices: Establish incident command structure immediately - unclear ownership delays resolution
Communicate proactively and frequently - stakeholders prefer updates over silence
Focus on service restoration first, root cause analysis second during active incidents
Document everything in real-time - timelines and decisions are harder to reconstruct later
antiPatterns: Blaming individuals in post-mortems - focus on systems and processes instead
Skipping incident command for 'everyone responds' - causes coordination chaos
Delaying communication to have complete information - stakeholders need timely updates
Implementing complex fixes during active incidents - prefer minimal viable fixes

faq.question: How quickly should I respond to a P0 incident?
faq.answer: P0 (critical) incidents require acknowledgment within 15 minutes and resolution within 1 hour. Immediate escalation and incident command establishment are critical.

faq.question: What is the difference between incident commander and technical lead?
faq.answer: Incident Commander makes decisions, coordinates response, manages communication. Technical Lead investigates the technical root cause and implements fixes. Separate roles prevent cognitive overload.

faq.question: How often should I send incident updates?
faq.answer: For active incidents: every 15 minutes for P0/P1, hourly for P2. Updates should include current status, actions taken, next steps, and ETA if known.

faq.question: When should I declare an incident resolved?
faq.answer: Declare resolution when all SLIs return to normal thresholds, user experience is validated, and capacity headroom is confirmed. Continue enhanced monitoring for 24 hours post-resolution.

faq.question: How do I conduct a blameless post-mortem?
faq.answer: Focus on what happened and why, not who made mistakes. Use techniques like five whys or fishbone diagrams. Identify systemic factors, not human errors. Share learnings openly.

faq.question: Can this skill execute actual remediation commands?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides guidance and recommendations only. It cannot access your systems, execute commands, or make decisions. Always verify guidance against your specific environment.
